FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD cohort (U.S. ABCD Study, NIH-funded), n=7471 young adolescents, ABCD Data Release 5.0.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models, PLSR, mediation analysis on ABCD neuroimaging, intelligence, and lifestyle data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Translational Psychiatry (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient found in any results location. The only screen variable is a single-item generic “screen use” (average hours/day of screen use from ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire); label is not phone/SM-specific. Step 2: The methods describe screen use as “average hours an adolescent used screen in a day” — a generic single item with no description of exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing content; bundles all screen types without per-device/platform breakout. Fails Step 2.

notes: Screen use variable (β=-0.509 for fluid intelligence, β=-0.699 for crystallized intelligence) is generic daily screen hours from the ABCD STQ, not isolable as phone/SM-specific. No per-platform or per-device breakout found in main text, tables, or supplements.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses data from 11,875 U.S. adolescents in the ABCD study (NDA Release 3.0); confirms U.S. recruitment from 21 sites, ages 9–11.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: generalized additive mixed models and linear mixed-effects models on ABCD MRI and cognitive data, ROI and vertexwise analyses.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Scientific Reports (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable found in any results location. The paper examines only (a) daily pleasurable reading hours and (b) daily television viewing hours as IVs. Neither is a phone/SM-specific variable. No texting, social media time, named SM platform, app use, smartphone ownership, or any other phone/SM measure appears in any table or analysis. Step 2: No composite to evaluate — the exposures are reading and TV viewing, neither of which is phone/SM-based.

notes: Paper focuses entirely on reading and television viewing; neither qualifies as a phone/SM variable under IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the ABCD Study® (Data Release 5.1; 2016–2022), ~11,800 U.S. adolescents ages 9–10 at baseline, 21 sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: mixed-effects linear modeling on prospective ABCD longitudinal data (baseline through 3-year follow-up) examining ACEs → PSMU and moderation by emotional/behavioral dysregulation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Issues in Mental Health Nursing (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: PSMU (Problematic Social Media Use) measured by the SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire, 6-item) is the DV — a qualifying SM variable. ACEs (cumulative adverse childhood experiences score) is the IV — a qualifying individual-trait-level predictor (trauma exposure). Table 2 reports β=1.5, p<0.001 for high (≥4) ACEs → higher PSMU at Y3. Phone/SM-specific qualifying variable (SMAQ) paired with individual-trait-level predictor (ACEs) found in main results table.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports β=1.5 (95% CI: 1.09–1.88), p=0.001 for high ACEs → PSMU; β=0.23 (95% CI: -0.10–0.58), p=0.180 for medium ACEs; moderation models (Table 3) report multiple coefficients. Quantitative inferential results present.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as FT_IC3: mixed-effects model with numeric regression coefficients (β, 95% CI, p-values) reported for the ACEs → PSMU relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) longitudinal design — ACEs measured at Y1 predicting PSMU at Y3; (b) PSMU/SMAQ is the DV in every analysis (main model and all moderation models); (c) no analysis anywhere in the paper uses SM/PSMU as IV. No cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV was found in main text, tables, or supplements, so the cross-sectional carve-out does not apply.
notes: Paper excluded under FT-EC7. PSMU (SMAQ) is DV throughout; ACEs as IV qualifies as individual-trait (passes IC2) but design is longitudinal DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere, and no cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis to trigger the carve-out.
